author: @ryanbrown982
text:
I remember 25 years ago being in middle school art class and listening to a *science* teacher talk to the art teacher about how the pennies they were using in a buoyancy experiment were all white and didn't get clean when she soaked them in water. I couldn't believe my 13 year old ears that the *science* teacher didn't realize that the pennies had oxidized.

I told her to soak them briefly in salt and vinegar and then wipe them off and that they'd come out with a mirror finish.

author: @lazyj944
text:
I use molasses/water mix and phosphoric acid/water mix to remove rust.
You have to do test pieces as they will disolve some netals but they work well, also the phosphoric acid leaves a protective coating.
The mixtures can be found on YouTube videos.

replies.author: @RandomAxeOfKindness
replies.text: I still prefer EvapoRust, but I remain astonished by how well molasses will remove rust.

author: @tonymercer265
text: I stuck some Velcro to a stainless tub, because my sander uses Velcro pads.

author: @hahaha9076
text: White vinegar on it's own for 24 hrs. Just move the parts around with a little shake by hand 4 or 5 times. No need to vibrate or add anything else.

author: @peterwall8191
text: 1:12 Not salt dude! It 'll pit the metal! Palin table vinegar will do. Follow it up with a soda and water wipe,to neutralise the acid left on the metal, oil slightly if you 're OCD enough. Usually .. i oil. why the hell not? A pair of cheap latex gloves, an old cloth, some oil, a bit of your time..if it'll keep rust away its worth the time.

author: @TrueSighted
text:
Have you ever considered a project where you build and tune an ultrasonic generator from off the shelf or spare parts and other accessable technology? I've seen a few things on making ultrasonic generators and then trying to tune them to create a good focal range for the cavitation, but it looked really tricky.

 I'd be curious to see what take you'd have on it. You seem to have a nack of making complex concept's and engineering seem easier to understand.

author: @johnjones4825
text: I restore an antique dresser last year, it has steel handles and mirror clips, all well rusted. A 48 hour soak in neat vinegar took off enough rust to leave a nice antique patina. A light coat of clear, matt varnish protects from further deterioration.

author: @rastamanralph6670
text: When I'm panning for gold I use a surfacter to break the water tension because believe it or not gold floats! I was thinking you probably won't need to use the alcohol which is easier for people who can't get a hold of if like me.

author: @markward251
text: Another great vid from Robert. It's clear his solution works very well and I'll definately give it a go. Using my very limited knowledge of chemistry: reactive properties of an alkaline (eg salt) and acid (eg vinegar) when mixed in the right amounts cancelled each other out. (If correct) This would imply that mixing the two would reduce the cleaning effect of the solution. I think Rob's demonstration proves my posit is incorrect but I wonder how this works at a molecular level?

author: @martinwinfield2935

author: @MikeGrayM70
text: I've got an old rock tumbler that I want to try this out on. I cleaned out rust inside a motorcycle fuel tank once by loading it with some gravel, wrapping it up in cushions, and tumbling it in my clothes dryer for a couple hours. Removed almost all the rust. I was able to use a lot less chemical treatment as a result. :)

author: @PACstove
text: Nice recipe. Gonna use it Thanks!  I wanted to share back mine.  Maybe someone is already doing it, but i think my recipe is unique.  I'm using rusty bits like you have there, or metal filings dropped into a large bottle of vinegar.  Let it sit a few days before ready to use.  When you are ready to stain some wood add one package of cherry koolaid. No sugar, just the packet.   That's IT!   It makes a wonderful and cheap stain for outdoor wood, and actually gets better with age in some cases.  I've experimented with adding old coffee grounds to throw color off and add some oils.

author: @groovedodger

author: @gilbertsprojects2954
text: My dad always saved every nut bolt and washer from anything he disassembled and stored them in trays with a light coat of oil... never needed to buy any nuts bolts or washers for anything he put together. We live in a far too “throw away” society today but I’m following his footsteps and it’s saved me lots of money and time. 👍🏼

replies.author: @markiobook8639
replies.text: same. When hard rubbish collection day came around ot was Christmas for him and hours of disassembly for us kids.

replies.author: @seattlecarpenter
replies.text: I'm the same way at work. It feels wrong to throw away something that I will need 2 years down the line!

replies.author: @johndias6614
replies.text: This is good to a point. If the fastener has been abused in its previous life, there's a chance it could fail.

replies.author: @gilbertsprojects2954
replies.text: @johndias6614 yes agreed, I wouldn’t use second hand fasteners on something critical but for general ‘bolting things together’ it saves money and time 👍🏼

author: @benthere8051
text: That's very useful, Robert. Would an ultrasonic cleaner have the same effect or does the vibratory cleaner work better? Thanks for your very useful video.

replies.author: @stevecummins324
replies.text: I believe ultrasonic cleaners, are supposed to create cavitation bubbles next to part being cleaned.. when cavitation bubbles collapse in effect they're like tiny explosions. Cavitation can erode very hard materials... so ultrasonic cleaning probably a harsher process.

replies.author: @jesusliveselima3846
replies.text: I can see everybody's asking for ideas and if you look up ultrasonic dishwashers also jewelry cleaner machines friends of mine use it for carburetor carburetors to be disassemble and cleaned and it does work good doesn't harm the surface of anything many uses with that especially vaporizing or making a gas like a fog you could do a lot with a vaporizer ultrasonic look it up you'll find a lot of uses for it and it will fit this application

text: Remember to protect your freshly cleaned items as they will rust up really quickly.Oil etc

author: @Aethalops
text: Very interesting and helpful. How do you dispose of the cleaning solution after you're done?

replies.author: @paulmaydaynight9925
replies.text: in this case its a mild acid and water, so down the drain, or just coffee filter it into a bucket to clean it up and reuse it over, throwing away perfectly good materials you use just wastes your money

replies.author: @anthonybailey7628
replies.text: @paulmaydaynight9925 yes, but it’s an oily emulsion isn’t it? I’d think twice about pouring oil emulsion down the drain. What I do is degrease in some sort of organic solvent, then when it’s too oil laden, mix it with the old engine oil which my auto parts store takes for free and disposes of safely.
